text: We present our experience with laparoscopic radical nephrectomy with inferior vena cava thrombectomy. We present the case of a sixty five year old male patient with no relevant clinical history.

He complained of progressive loss of weight up to ten kilograms, edema in both lower limbs, and denied hematuria or pain. CT scan of abdomen and pelvis showed a solid infiltrating mass occupying the middle and lower third of the right kidney, which enhanced with contrast. Invasion of the right renal vein and inferior vena cava below the diaphragm was present.

The patient is positioned in lateral decubitus, and three trocars are inserted along the midline: two 12mm trocars and one 5mm trocar. An additional 12mm subcostal trocar is placed for surgical access.
The surgeon releases the lower border of the liver, exposing the inferior vena cava for identification.

The surgeon dissects the right renal artery at the intercaval aortic level and applies early clamping. Four hepatic veins, tributaries of the caudate lobe, are identified on the anterior face of the inferior vena cava, which are then ligated and sectioned.
The surgeon progresses above the upper limit of the thrombus in the inferior vena cava and places a Rommel's tourniquet around the vessel, adjusting it to control blood flow.
The surgeon removes the vascular clamps from the previously clamped vessels, completing the step of releasing the vascular structures.
In conclusion, laparoscopic radical nephrectomy with inferior vena cava thrombectomy is a challenging but feasible procedure in selected patients.
